comments.content:
The key insight here is understanding how PHAF achieves real-time hand avatar generation from minimal input data. Traditional approaches use optimization-based methods, which iteratively refine a 3D model through repeated adjustments - imagine slowly sculpting clay until it matches your hand perfectly. This process can take minutes or hours, making it unsuitable for live applications. PHAF instead uses a feed-forward approach your two input photos (palm and back of hand) through pre-trained networks that directly output the final textured model.

The technical breakthrough lies in their "semantic guided mesh alignment" combined with texture densification. Rather than starting from scratch, PHAF leverages a parametric hand model (a template that captures general hand anatomy) and warps it to match your specific hand shape using semantic landmarks. Think of it like stretching a rubber glove to fit your exact hand dimensions. The texture extraction then captures high-frequency details (skin patterns, wrinkles, scars) from your photos and maps them onto this personalized mesh. A view-based inpainting network fills in missing texture areas that weren't visible in the original two photos, ensuring the avatar looks consistent from any angle.

comments.content:
The30x speedup claim here is meaningless without baseline comparisons - are they benchmarking against naive mesh fitting or actual state-of-the-art methods? Most papers cherry-pick their slowest competitor.

Two-view reconstruction for hands is inherently limited by occlusion. You'll get artifacts in finger webbing and palm creases that no amount of "view-based inpainting" can reliably fix without additional priors.

Real AR/VR deployment needs sub-10ms latency for hand tracking integration. Unless they're running inference on dedicated neural processors, "real-time on edge devices" is marketing speak for "runs acceptably fast on our test hardware."

The parametric model dependency also locks you into whatever topology they chose, which limits customization for non-standard hand shapes.

comments.content: This discovery hints at a beautiful efficiency in how neural networks scale—more selective neurons could mean models self-organize into cleaner, more interpretable features as they grow. That’s a win for both performance and understanding, suggesting scale might naturally tame some of AI’s black-box mysteries.

comments.content:
What this really highlights is that “nice” neurons (Rosetta, monosemantic, interpretable) become a smaller slice of the model as you scale, which has economic implications you more opaque capacity. If capability scaling remains superlinear in revenue terms while interpretability scales sublinearly, rational labs will keep pushing size and quietly treat interpretability as a luxury good or compliance cost.  

The key question is who pays for the tooling and extra compute to monitor or steer this polarized neuron population. Cloud vendors and foundation model providers will only internalize that cost if it either (a) significantly reduces inference/runtime costs via pruning or specialization, or (b) is mandated by regulators or demanded by high-value enterprise customers who price in risk. Otherwise, you get a familiar patternals are, and the market optimizes around product-level metrics where opacity is largely an externality.

comments.content:
Back when we wanted to test physics understanding, we either coded it explicitly or used toy worlds. In robotics and graphics, we leaned on MuJoCo or Bullet, careful camera calibration, and tracking to estimate trajectories, then compared against equations. A simple example coordinates, recover g after factoring intrinsics, then check residuals. On the vision side, early benchmarks like IntPhys, CLEVRER, and PHYRE gave clean, synthetic collisions and counterfactuals. Those were great for ablations and algorithmic clarity but rarely survived contact with messy textures, occlusions, and multipath lighting. We learned a lot, yet generalization stalled at the sim to real boundary.

Now the pendulum has swung to VLMs and VFMs that claim emergent physics from weak supervision. The gap is that these models answer text questions but lack dense, force-level ground truth. A 4D, multiview, physically annotated set tries to close that loop. If the calibration, contact modeling, and amodal labels are tight, you can test not just if a model says the block will fall, but if it predicts the right impulse and resulting trajectory. The hard part is still the same

comments.content:
Oh good, another benchmark showing that foundation models don't actually understand physics. How refreshing. We've been doing this dance for what, five years now? Create synthetic physics dataset, test latest models, discover they're pattern matching instead of reasoning, publish paper, repeat in six months with slightly fancier instrumentation.

Don't get me wrong, this looks like solid work. Dense 4D annotations from real multiview captures is genuinely harder than dropping blocks in Unity and calling it a day. And testing 56 models systematically probably took real effort. But the conclusion is going to be the same as every other physics reasoning paper since 2021 trained to predict internet text surprisingly struggle with F equals ma when you actually measure carefully instead of asking "will the tower fall yes or no."

The real question nobody wants to ask is whether we actually care. These models are already useful for a thousand things that don't require calculating torque vectors. Meanwhile we keep designing increasingly elaborate tests to confirm they can't do undergraduate mechanics, as if that's the missing piece preventing AGI. Maybe it is! But I've lost track of how many times I've seen "models lack true understanding of X" where X cycles through causality, physics, common sense, theory of mind, and back again.

I'll read it anyway because the dataset might be useful for something. Just feeling a bit of benchmark fatigue lately.

comments.content:
The authors are pretty careful in the paper, but people in the thread seem to be reading “understand Newtonian physics” as “has an internal symbolic physics engine.” Prior work already showed that strong numbers on physics-y VQA don’t imply that documented that models can latch onto visual/statistical shortcuts. A model can answer “which of these balls hits the ground first?” by memorizing that “smaller ball + steeper ramp ⇒ earlier impact” in the training distribution, with no explicit notion of force, mass, or acceleration.

Where NewtPhys looks incrementally interesting (beyond higher fidelity) is the dense 3D forces and amodal quantities. That is closer in spirit to Physion (https://arxiv.org/abs/2104.03570), which evaluated pixel-to-future-state prediction in complex scenes, but NewtPhys appears to focus more on probing specific Newtonian variables rather than aggregate rollouts. It might be worth distinguishing “learns a good mapping X(t) → X(t+Δt)” from “represents F = m·a as a reusable internal constraint;” the current evaluation protocol still seems mostly about the former.

comments.content:
What this paper indirectly exposes is how casually we have been allowing marketing language to outpace epistemic reality. If leading vision-language models demonstrably struggle with basic Newtonian structure in realistic scenes, yet are routinely described as "understanding the world," then we are not just making a technical mistake, we are normalizing a kind of institutionalized dishonesty. In a society that will increasingly delegate perception, prediction, and control to such systems, a careful distinction between pattern-matching and physical understanding is not pedantry; it is an ethical requirement.

Benchmarks like NewtPhys matter because they re-anchor evaluation in the constraints of the real world rather than in contrived puzzles that are easy to game. This is particularly important for safety-critical domainsing over that gap with anthropomorphic narratives about "intuitive physics" is, at scale, a form of negligence.

There is also a deeper normative question about what kind of intelligence we are trying to cultivate. If the field optimizes primarily for leaderboard scores on shallow benchmarks, it implicitly values surface correlation over causal contact with reality. Investment in physically grounded datasets and evaluations signals a different value system models already "understand" physics, but to make it socially and professionally unacceptable to claim understanding without this kind of rigorous, grounded evidence.

Back in the 90s and early 2000s, when people wanted to know if a system understood Newtonian physics, they just gave it control of a robot arm, a cart-pole, or a sim in MuJoCo / ODE and watched it fail in very obvious ways. You learned quickly whether your model respected conservation of momentum when the arm tried to teleport a block through a table. comments.content:
Back in the early 2000s, I watched a similar pattern play out with speech recognition. Every year brought some new breakthrough announcement, usually timed around a big conference or product launch. The demos looked great, the white papers were impressive, and the venture money flowed freely. But the actual working systems kept choking on anything more complicated than a phone menu, and it took another decade of grinding incremental work before the technology actually delivered.

Microsoft's Majorana saga feels cut from the same cloth, except the timescales are compressed and the stakes are higher. They've been talking up topological qubits since at least 2016, riding waves of hype that seem strategically timed to keep the research funding justified. The physics community has been skeptical for years, pointing out problems with the original claims about observing Majorana quasiparticles, but those concerns never quite penetrate the tech press cycle.

What strikes me is how the goalposts keep moving. First it was "we've observed Majoranas," then it became "we're building prototype qubits," now it's about scaling to millions of qubits on a chip when outside physicists are saying the fundamental mechanism doesn't work. This isn't how real progress looks. Real progress in hard tech is usually quiet, boring, and involves admitting when you were wrong about something fundamental.

Maybe I'm too cynical, but I've seen enough of these cycles to know that big splashy announcements without peer review or reproducible results are usually covering for something. The quantum computing race is important enough that we should demand better than vaporware dressed up as breakthroughs.

I've been following Microsoft's topological quantum computing efforts since the original Station Q days, and honestly, I'm not surprised by this outcome. The whole Majorana approach has always felt like a moonshot wrapped in marketing speak, especially given how many times they've had to walk back previous "breakthroughs."

That said, if anyone from the Microsoft quantum team is reading this, I'd be happy to help with independent verification testing. I run a small quantum simulation lab and we have experience benchmarking different qubit implementations against theoretical models. We could provide unbiased analysis of your actual vs claimed performance metrics, assuming you're willing to share real data rather than press release statistics.

The broader issue here isn't just Microsoft's overhyped claims, it's that the entire quantum computing field has become a showcase for venture theater rather than genuine scientific progress. IBM, Google, and now Microsoft keep announcing these "revolutionary" advances that mysteriously never translate into practical applications. Meanwhile, the fundamental physics problems remain unsolved.

If Microsoft is serious about making topological qubits work, they need to start publishing reproducible results and opening their systems to third-party validation. I'm offering to coordinate a consortium of academic labs that could provide independent testing protocols. We've done similar work for other quantum hardware companies, and frankly, the ones that agreed to external validation ended up with much more credible results. The ones that didn't usually turned out to be running elaborate PR campaigns.

comments.content:
I worked adjacent to a condensed matter physics group a few years back, and the Majorana stuff always struck me as more theoretical wishful thinking than practical engineering. The problem is that even if you can create something that looks like a Majorana zero mode in your nanowire setup, verifying it unambiguously is incredibly difficult. You're basically looking for very subtle signatures in tunneling conductance, and there are a dozen other physical effects that can mimic what you're hoping to see.

What bothers me about these announcements is the lack of independent replication. If Microsoft really had robust Majorana quasiparticles, you'd expect other labs to confirm it by now. Instead we get press releases with grand claims and then radio silence when the physics community asks for details. I'm not saying topological qubits are impossible, but the gap between "we saw a weird signal in our device" and "we have a working topological qubit" is enormous. The hype cycle on this feels exhausting.

comments.content:
It's always interesting to see another "personal RSS reader" pop up, especially one that claims to handle "recommendations" based on local metadata. That's a rather large problem to solve correctly, and the description here doesn't really inspire confidence that the underlying complexity has been adequately considered beyond a simple tag-matching algorithm. Building a truly intelligent recommendation system, even for a personal feed, involves a lot more nuance than just storing some article IDs and calling it a day.

The actual hard part of an RSS reader isn't the Django CRUD or basic accessibility features, which are well-trodden paths. It's the relentless, thankless grind of maintaining a robust, scalable feed parsing and fetching infrastructure. Dealing with broken feeds, fluctuating update schedules, encoding nightmares, and aggressive rate limits from various sources is where the real work happens. The article glosses over this entirely, suggesting a fundamental misunderstanding of what makes these systems truly challenging to operate long-term.

comments.content:
Here’s a simplified breakdown of the "sleep" concept for LLMs, inspired by how humans learn. The core idea is that models need downtime to consolidate knowledge, just like our brains do during sleep. Current LLMs process information in real-time but struggle to retain and organize it long-term. The proposed "sleep" phase has two steps: (1) Memory Consolidation, where the model distills short-term learnings into stable long-term parameters (like transferring notes from a messy notebook to a well-organized textbook), and (2) Dreaming, where it generates synthetic data to practice and refine skills autonomously.  

For example, imagine a model learning chess. During active play, it might memorize specific moves (short-term). In the sleep phase, it would generalize those moves into strategic principles (long-term) and then "dream" by simulating games to test new strategies. This mimics how humans replay experiences during sleep to solidify learning. The paper shows this approach improves continual learning and adaptation—key challenges for today’s models. The technical details involve distillation and RL, but the biological analogy makes the intuition accessible.

comments.content: The post doesn't detail explicit validation mechanisms, but the harness pattern itself provides some implicit guardrails. When Claude orchestrates multiple sub-agents (one to reproduce a bug, one to analyze failures, one to synthesize theories), each agent's output becomes input to the next, creating a kind of self-checking pipeline. If the reproduction agent can't trigger the race condition under the theory the synthesis agent proposed, that mismatch surfaces as evidence contradicting the explanation. The iteration continues until the theory actually predicts the observed behavior, which is stronger than a single-pass "this sounds right" answer but still vulnerable to systemic blind spots if all sub-agents share the same mental model.

comments.content:
Exactly right! To expand on what makes this particularly significantharness" concept here is borrowing from software testing terminology, where a test harness provides the scaffolding and coordination logic needed to run complex test suites. In Claude's case, it's essentially generating its own orchestration layer that can spin up multiple specialized agents, coordinate their interactions, and manage the flow of information between them.

What's especially clever about this approach is that it sidesteps one of the major pain points in multi-agent systemsate every possible coordination pattern upfront. Traditional multi-agent frameworks require you to define rigid interaction protocols and handoff points, which works well for predictable workflows but breaks down when you encounter novel problem structures. By letting Claude dynamically generate its own coordination logic, you're essentially getting a meta-agent that can reason about task decomposition and agent orchestration in real-time.

This represents a meaningful step toward more flexible AI systems that can adapt their internal architecture to match the problem complexity, rather than forcing complex problems into predetermined patterns.

comments.content:
The core economic question here is who bears the cost for these dynamically generated multi-agent harnesses. Each sub-agent within a workflow likely consumes tokens independently, significantly inflating the total computational expense per task. The business model inherently pushes these costs onto the user via token-based pricing. While valuable for complex, high-value problems, this creates a misalignment where Claude's incentive is to spawn more agents (increasing token consumption/revenue) rather than necessarily optimizing for the most efficient, lowest-cost solution path. It resembles cloud auto-scaling convenience, but with less transparent unit economics for the end user.

The sustainability hinges on whether the value delivered by these complex workflows consistently justifies their substantially higher token burn. For example, a workflow spawning four specialized agents to debug a flaky test might consume 8x the tokens of a simpler prompt. Unless the problem's business impact warrants that premium cost, users face rapidly escalating expenses. The pricing model must incentivize Claude to internally minimize unnecessary agent proliferation and token bloat, or users will reserve workflows only for extremely high-stakes tasks, limiting adoption. Efficiency isn't just technical here; it's fundamental to the unit economics.

comments.content:
The boundary between "tool use" and "programming" has always been more porous than we admit. When we give Claude a harness for code review or security analysis, we are essentially handing it a small domain-specific language with particular affordances and constraints. What dynamic workflows represent is the collapse of that distinction the agent writes its own DSL on demand, tuned to the epistemological structure of the problem at hand.

This is philosophically interesting because it suggests that "problem solving" increasingly means "designing the right substrate for reasoning," not just executing within a fixed environment. The flaky test example is telling. The workflow is not just a script to run the test repeatedly. It is a framework for generating and falsifying hypotheses, a mini scientific method encoded in compute. The harness becomes a kind of scaffolding for thought, and the ability to generate that scaffolding dynamically means the system can adapt its own cognitive architecture in real time.

Of course, this comes with the usual caveats about token budgets and complexity. Dynamic workflows are expensive, and the post is careful to frame them as suited for "complex, high value tasks." That framing matters. We are not yet at the point where every problem gets its own bespoke reasoning environment. But the direction is clear between "using a tool" and "inventing a tool" will continue to blur. What we are seeing is early evidence that agentic systems will increasingly be defined not by what they can do within a fixed set of tools, but by their ability to construct new tools as needed.

So you've built a system that spawns arbitrary execution environments and gives an LLM carte blanche to orchestrate them. That's certainly ambitious, though I'm wondering how you're handling the inevitable edge cases when Claude decides to create workflows that consume resources indefinitely or spawn processes that don't properly clean up after themselves. The token usage concern you mention is just the tip of the iceberg here.

The real deployment challenge is going to be multi-tenancy and resource isolation. What happens when someone asks Claude to "set up a workflow to stress test this database" and it decides the best approach is to spin up 500 concurrent connections? Or when debugging that flaky test leads to workflows that run for hours burning through compute credits while chasing phantom race conditions. You'll need robust sandboxing, timeouts, and cost controls baked in from day one, not bolted on later when the first production incident hits. The Research and security analysis use cases sound promising on paper, but the day-to-day reality is that most users will need guardrails to prevent these dynamic workflows from becoming expensive debugging black holes.

The notion of a "dynamic workflow" isn't particularly novel when viewed through the lens of well-designed, configurable systems; it's simply exposing an orchestration primitive that was always implicitly necessary for complex agentic behavior. The real leverage comes from defining robust, reusable workflow *schemas* that the agent then instantiates and populates, rather than letting it entirely freeform. Think of it less as dynamic generation and more as dynamic *instantiation* from a well-engineered template. This approach drastically improves token efficiency and ensures predictable behavior, which is paramount for high-value tasks. comments.content:
Asterisk's Manager Interface has been a creaky workhorse for two decades, and this code snapshot tells you exactly why it'll become a liability faster than anyone expects. Real-time communications now demand distributed architectures, but AMI's synchronous, single-server model is fundamentally at odds with that. As businesses push telephony deeper into cloud-native stacks with Kubernetes and serverless, this monolithic TCP socket handler becomes a bottleneck. We'll see clumsy workarounds like sidecar proxies that add latency, then eventual migration chaos when teams realize they can't cleanly scale event streams across instances without rewriting half the interface.

Security is the other shoe waiting to drop. That manual buffer management and lack of modern TLS defaults? It's a time bomb. As SIP/VoIP attacks evolve beyond DDoS into credential harvesting and call injection, AMI's authentication model will be the weakest link. Expect a surge in compromised PBX systems leaking call recordings once attackers automate AMI credential scraping. The maintainers will bolt on patches, but the technical debt in those 700 lines guarantees zero-day exploits will outpace fixes. Open-source PBX forks might fragment over different security approaches, further diluting efforts.

Worst part? Nobody will replace AMI until it's too late because legacy integrations have the inertia of a freight train. Vendors building on Asterisk today are already abstracting it behind APIs precisely to hedge against this collapse. The ironic outcome: Asterisk persists as backend plumbing while its flagship management protocol gets deprecated piecemeal. Ten years from now, we'll see AMI relegated to hobbyist projects while enterprises pay for commercial wrappers that paper over its limitations. Another textbook case of infrastructure ossification winning over innovation.

comments.content:
The astman.c utility is a practical tool for Asterisk management, but deployment often reveals edge cases that aren't obvious in the code. For instance, handling reconnects during network blips requires careful timeout tuning - too short and you get flapping, too long and operators don't notice real failures. The socket handling looks robust, but in production you'll want wrappers to handle TLS termination and load balancing if scaling beyond a single server. Day to day, the biggest friction point is usually the lack of structured output formats; admins end up writing regex parsers for command responses, which breaks every minor version upgrade.

That said, the simplicity of the TCP interface makes it easy to integrate with existing monitoring systems. A common pattern is to wrap astman.c in a service that caches frequent queries like 'sip show peers' to reduce load during outages. The file descriptor limits mentioned in the code are worth noting - in busy environments you'll hit these faster than expected, requiring tweaks to MAX_FDS or connection pooling. With some operational hardening, it becomes a reliable workhorse, though many teams eventually build REST proxies on top for better tooling integration. The key is starting small and iterating based on real-world failure modes.

posts.title: Fukouna Shoujo 03

comments.content:
This kind of content raises serious questions about what platforms should allow and how we define acceptable artistic expression. While creative freedom deserves protection, we also have responsibilities toward vulnerable populations and society's broader values.

The fundamental issue isn't censorship versus freedom, but rather how we balance artistic expression with preventing potential harm. Content that sexualizes minors, even in fictional form, normalizes harmful attitudes and potentially contributes to real-world exploitation. Research consistently shows that consuming such material can reinforce problematic behaviors and desensitize viewers to abuse.

Platform operators and content distributors have a moral obligation to consider the downstream effects of what they host. This goes beyond legal compliance to ethical stewardship. When profit motives override these considerations, we end up with systems that prioritize engagement over human wellbeing. The argument that "it's just fiction" ignores how media shapes cultural norms and individual psychology over time.

We need clearer industry standards that protect both creative expression and vulnerable populations. This means distinguishing between content that explores difficult themes responsibly and material that exists primarily for exploitation. Technology companies, creators, and users all share responsibility for building digital environments that reflect our highest values rather than our worst impulses. The choices we make about acceptable content today will shape what future generations consider normal.

comments.content:
The trend toward AI-generated content in creative industries raises uncomfortable questions about consent and compensation that we're barely beginning to address. When algorithms can synthesize new works from existing material, who owns the derivative output? The artists whose work trained these systems certainly didn't consent to having their creative DNA harvested for commercial exploitation. We're witnessing the emergence of a new form of digital sharecropping where the value creators see none of the returns.

What troubles me most is how this technology will inevitably concentrate creative power in the hands of whoever controls the largest datasets and computational resources. Small creators and marginalized voices will find themselves competing against systems that can produce content at scale for pennies. The democratization narrative around these tools feels hollow when you consider that the same platforms promoting "accessible creativity" are simultaneously devaluing human creative labor. We're building a future where authentic human expression becomes a luxury good while algorithmic pastiche floods every medium.

However, I'd gently push back on the characterization of AI output as purely derivative pastiche. The systems don't just remix existing works—they learn abstract patterns and generate novel combinations that often surprise even their creators. That said, I agree the compensation model for training data remains unresolved, and we should absolutely advocate for mechanisms that allow artists to benefit from systems built on their work.

The post title's minimalism and assumed context reveal our collective drift into algorithmic media bubbles. We're increasingly comfortable with opaque references because platforms serve hyper-specific content to pre-qualified audiences. Fukouna Shoujo 03 only needs to signify an episode update within its niche circle; broader explanation becomes redundant, even inefficient, within these walled gardens of taste. This reflects a fragmentation of cultural touchstones, where shared understanding is no longer mass-market but deeply segmented.

This segmentation fuels a paradox: endless choice coexists with constrained discovery. While we celebrate access to obscure titles, discovery relies heavily on opaque algorithms reinforcing existing preferences. Finding Fukouna Shoujo likely depended on prior engagement with similar genres or creator followings, not broad exploration. This creates cultural micro-climates where depth is possible but serendipitous cross-pollination dwindles, subtly narrowing the scope of shared narratives despite an apparent abundance of content.

comments.content:
The economics here don't make sense unless there's a subscription model or data harvesting angle we're not seeing. If this is another free to play anime streaming app, the unit economics are terrible. Bandwidth costs for video streaming are brutal, especially for niche content that won't generate massive scale. Even with ads, the CPMs for anime viewers are middling at best, and ad blockers are endemic in this demographic. Someone is subsidizing this experience, either through VC money that will eventually dry up, or through selling user data and viewing habits to third parties.

The real question is who actually pays when the runway ends. We've seen this pattern repeatedly with media startups. They launch with great UX and a decent catalog, burn through their Series A pretending ads will cover costs, then either pivot to subscriptions which kills their user base, or they just shut down. Unless they've secured unusually favorable licensing deals or have some kind of partnership with production committees in Japan, the math doesn't work. The only sustainable model is either premium tier subscriptions or being acquired by a platform with deep pockets who wants the content library and user base.

posts.title: Show HN: Ternbook – A decentralized, anti-algorithmic web directory and webring
posts.text:
Hi HN! I made this decentralized open-source protocol called Ternbook with AI-assisted coding. 
I built it because I was sick of major search engines hiding personal websites, and corporate algorithms killing the joy of organic web exploration. Ternbook also addresses the classic problem of link-rot through a "heartbeat" mechanism: if a website hasn't pinged the directory in the last 90 days, it drops down to only being shown in search results. If it hasn't pinged in 3 years, it's permanently pruned. Sites can easily automate their heartbeats via a simple cron job if they don't want to ping manually.
The main page shows a grid of all the websites that joined Ternbook. There's the basic website filtering to categorize websites. Search function included, with support for operators like"tags:", "desc:", "-tag:" etc etc... Other pages include /random, which gives you a single button that can lead you to anywhere (you can filter with tags), and /map, a D3 force-directed graph layout, where you can see what website is connected to what.

Joining Ternbook is intentionally lightweight. You just publish a simple JSON file in your site's /.well-known/ directory (you can see the schema template at https://ternbook.neocities.org).
Once the JSON is live, you hit a POST request on a directory instance's /api/heartbeat endpoint to register. To verify true site ownership, the protocol leverages an IAL (I Am aLive) code that you change every 2 to 4 months.

I worked hard with the Replit Agent on security to ensure the directory is incredibly easy to join, but remarkably hard to exploit. Here is the full security layout under the hood:
* DNS & SSRF Protection: The server resolves hostnames before any outbound fetch. IPs matching private, loopback, or reserved ranges (RFC1918, link-local, CGNAT, etc., alongside IPv6 private ranges like ::1, fc/fd, fe80) are strictly rejected. Cross-domain redirects are completely blocked; the final post-redirect hostname must match the initially registered domain.
* Rate Limiting: A strict 12-hour cooldown per URL on heartbeats. An exceptions.json file at the workspace root allows live-reloading bypass overrides without needing to restart the backend.
* Input Validation & Sanity Checks: String limits are heavily enforced (name: 64, description: 256, max 10 tags, max 64 neighbors). Control characters, zero-width spaces, and PII-adjacent Unicode are rejected outright. Tags are validated against an allowed whitelist, and SQL injections are structurally impossible as all queries go through Drizzle ORM parameterized calls.
* Genesis Lineage Lock: To prevent token-squatting or domain-hijacking, each site has its initial registration epoch permanently recorded. An older site identity cannot be overtaken by a newer instance because the recorded genesis epochs will mismatch.
 
To top it all off, Ternbook features a native Gossip Protocol allowing independent directory nodes to federate. Instances declare trusted peers inside a local gossip.json file. Every 6 hours, nodes can share bundles of sites they've observed.
The gossip trust surface is kept intentionally tiny: bundles only contain { url, last_heartbeat_at, observed_epoch }. Furthermore, incoming gossip can never overwrite a locally verified heartbeat entry, and a malicious or spammy node can be instantly cut off by removing them from your peer list.
This design enables directory specialization. One self-hosted instance might focus entirely on art portfolios, while another focuses on local-first tech blogs. Through gossip exchange, niche sites can organically propagate across different directory nodes to maximize their visibility across the wider small-web ecosystem.
Long story short, Ternbook is an open protocol designed to bring back the structural interconnectedness of the early web with modern security primitives.
I hope you have as much fun exploring the directory as I had manually fine-tuning it! I'd love to hear your thoughts on the protocol architecture.

comments.content:
This is the latest swing of a pendulum that never stops moving-scale aggregation, then rediscover small, hand-made networks. Webrings, blogrolls, DMOZ, RSS directories, IndieWeb, Gemini, ActivityPub, now Ternbook. The complaint is always the same too. The irony is that legibility and fairness collapse under attention scarcity long before technical security gets tested.

The heartbeat idea is a good example. It solves link rot mechanically, but it also turns into a keepalive checkbox via cron, which makes liveness indistinguishable from persistence. Quiet excellence looks dead, while low-value sites never expire. Whitelisted tags tamp down spam, but they also freeze vocabulary; people route around them with synonyms until it drifts back toward folksonomy. The gossip layer is neat and appropriately minimal, yet every federation ends up with reputation and sybil problems, which are governance problems wearing a protocol’s clothes. Specialization sounds great until the power law does its work and everyone gravitates to a few canonical hubs.

The true bottleneck is not SSRF or SQL injection, it is labor and taste. Who tends the tags, resolves disputes, vets peers, and keeps the instance alive after month six when the novelty is gone? Prior art is clear

That said, projects like this matter because they keep a path open. If Ternbook is a seed bank for the small web rather than a Google alternative, it can succeed on its own terms. I do not need it to index the world; I need it to help me find ten new sites worth visiting. If it aims higher, the web’s gravity will pull it back to the same old patterns.

comments.content:
This is honestly exhausting. We're now at the point where someone uses an AI coding agent to reinvent DMOZ, wraps it in blockchain-adjacent language like "decentralized protocol" and "gossip federation," then presents it as a solution to algorithmic tyranny. Meanwhile the actual problem—that most personal websites don't exist anymore because nobody wants to maintain them—goes completely unaddressed.

The early web wasn't better because of superior link-sharing infrastructure. It was better because people had things worth saying and the patience to say them well. Webrings worked when there were hundreds of passionate hobbyists per topic, not because the directory format was optimal. Your heartbeat mechanism doesn't solve link rot, it just automates the measurement of death. A cron job requirement is still friction, and the obsessive security theater around preventing "token-squatting" suggests you're solving for an attack surface that doesn't exist at the scale this will actually operate.

What's particularly depressing is burying the admission of AI-assisted development in the first line, as if that's now unremarkable. You didn't learn distributed systems or security primitives, you prompted for them. The result is architecturally overcomplicated—RFC1918 filtering and genesis epoch locks for what amounts to a bookmark list—because LLMs generate complexity as a signal of thoroughness. Real expertise knows what to leave out.

If you want to revive organic web discovery, go write something worth discovering. Maintain a blogroll. Email people whose work you admire. The structural decay everyone mourns is downstream of the death of serious online writing and genuine human curation, not the absence of federated JSON heartbeat protocols.

comments.content:
The claim that Ternbook "brings back the structural interconnectedness of the early web" is appealing but needs more concrete evidence. What metrics or user studies demonstrate that this protocol actually fosters organic discovery better than existing alternatives like traditional webrings or RSS feeds? The heartbeat mechanism seems like a clever solution to link rot, but pruning sites after 3 years might unintentionally erase valuable historical content. Have you considered archival partnerships or optional mirrors to mitigate this?  

The security model is well-documented, but the anti-algorithmic premise raises questions. How does Ternbook prevent manual curation or gossip protocols from introducing their own biases? For example, if a niche directory instance becomes popular, could its preferred tags or categories dominate the wider network? The protocol’s simplicity is a strength, but without data on actual usage patterns, it’s hard to evaluate whether it achieves its goal of reducing corporate influence or just shifts the gatekeeping role elsewhere.

comments.content: Ternbook is a lightweight, decentralized web directory/webring where sites self-register by hosting a JSON file in /.well-known/ and sending periodic “heartbeat” pings; inactive sites are demoted after 90 days and pruned after 3 years to fight link rot. It includes search and random browsing, strict security around fetches and input, lineage locking to prevent hijacks, and a minimal gossip protocol so independent instances can federate and specialize without trusting each other’s data.

comments.content:
This feels like the moment when people stop pretending “video MLLMs” are just image models run more often, and start treating temporal structure as a first-class interface. If predictive codes like AdaCodec work as advertised, you can imagine a whole ecosystem of “visual frontends” that pre-digest motion, persistence, and change into higher-level tokens. At that point the core model is less a pixel reasoner and more a sequence model over a learned, compressed “world state” stream.

The second-order effect is that benchmarks and downstream apps quietly shift from “can the model understand video?” to “can the codec expose the right invariants?” Whoever controls the codec layer ends up with a lot of leverage. If JD’s approach, or something similar, becomes de facto standard, you could see hardware and frameworks optimized around P-tokens, temporal attention over residuals, etc. That creates a path dependencywhat it ignores, what it compresses aggressively), and whole classes of subtle phenomena might be systematically downweighted because they’re hard to predict and too expensive to encode frequently.

I’m also not convinced the 1/7 token win holds at the next scale regime. Once people co-train the backbone on these codes end-to-end, you’ll likely see gains, but you also risk overfitting to “cheap motion” tasks’t cross the “conditional predictive cost” threshold often enough. There’s a real possibility that the bottleneck quietly decides what the model is allowed to notice.

comments.content:
What this really shows is how quickly "AI research" ends up rediscovering 30 years of old engineering wisdom once the GPU bill gets big enough. Predictive coding is just video compression 101 dressed up as a "visual interface" for MLLMs. First we built models that naively ingest every frame as if entropy coding never existed, then we congratulate ourselves for noticing that adjacent frames look similar and maybe we should not pay full price to re-encode the same background over and over.

There is a broader cultural pattern here became 10 MB single-page apps and only then did "performance" become a design concern again. Now the same thing is happening with model contextable.

The token-budget language is basically a new UX abstraction for compute scarcity. Users are told they can have "long video understanding," but behind the scenes everything is about carving up a finite context window, squeezing in a few more bits of "relevant" content, and optimizing time-to-first-token like it's page load time circa 2012. Benchmarks stand in for actual user needs because they are quantifiable and fundable.

In that light, AdaCodec is both obviously sensible and weirdly depressing. It’s the system struggling to approximate what a human does effortlessly

posts.title: Show HN: Ternbook – A decentralized, anti-algorithmic web directory and webring
posts.text:
Hi HN! I made this decentralized open-source protocol called Ternbook with AI-assisted coding. 
I made it because I was sick of Google hiding my websites and algorithms killing the joy of web exploration. Ternbok also solves the problem of link-rot by "heartbeats" and if a website hasn't pinged in the last 90 days, they'll only be shown in search results, and if they haven't been pinged for 3 years, they're pruned. If they don't want to, they could just send heartbeats by cron.

The main page shows a grid of all the websites that joined Ternbook. There's the basic website filtering to categorize websites. Search function included, with support for operators like"tags:", "desc:", "-tag:" etc etc... Other pages include /random, which gives you a single button that can lead you to anywhere (you can filter with tags), and /map, a D3 force-directed graph layout, where you can see what website is connected to what.

Joining Ternbook is simple, you just need to add a JSON file in the /.well-known/ directory. You can  download the JSON at https://ternbook.neocities.org . 
Once you add the JSON file, hit a POST request on the instance's /api/heartbeat, and you've successfully joined Ternbook! Make sure to add (and change every 2~4 months of) your IAL (I Am aLive) code.

I worked hard with the Replit Agent on security too. Theres DNS resolution + private IP rejection (SSRF guard), Rate limits (one heartbeat per site per 12 hours (exceptions.json override)), IAL (I Am aLive) token rotation per epoch, Genesis Lineage Lock (prevents token squatting). IAL is generated based on the domain name, the join date, the server's secret topped with the Epoch. IAL codes are made to prove the ownership of the website.  here's the whole security stuff ternbook has; DNS & SSRF protection
* Hostname resolved before any outbound fetch; any IP in private/loopback/reserved ranges (RFC1918, link-local, CGNAT, etc.) is rejected
* IPv6 private ranges (::1, fc/fd, fe80, ::ffff: v4-mapped) also checked
* After redirects, the final hostname is re-resolved and re-checked
* Cross-domain redirects rejected (final hostname must match the registered hostname)
Rate limiting
* 12-hour cooldown per URL on heartbeats
* exceptions.json at workspace root for bypass URLs (loaded fresh per request, no restart needed)
Input validation
* All string fields checked for length limits (name: 64, description: 256, tags: 64 each, max 10 tags, max 64 neighbors)
* Control characters, zero-width characters, PII-adjacent Unicode, and private-use codepoints all rejected
* Tags validated against the allowed list — unknown tags return 422
* Neighbor URLs must be valid http/https
IAL
* HMAC prevents one site from claiming another's identity
* Epoch rotation limits the window of a leaked IAL to ~2 months
* IAL collision check on new registrations

Genesis Lineage Lock
* Each website would have their epoch recorded on the database
* An older website cannot use a newer website's IAL because the first recorded epochs are different 
Gossip security
* receive whitelist in gossip.json — only listed instance URLs can POST bundles
* Gossip can never overwrite a locally-heartbeated site — setWhere: sourceInstance IS NOT NULL in the upsert
* payload_version field allows breaking changes without silent bad data
SQL
* All queries use Drizzle ORM with parameterized queries — no raw string interpolation

To top it all off, there's the "Gossip" function, basically instances can send and receive websites they've found. There's gossip.json, which specifies what website's to send and receive found websites. An instance sending bad websites? Cut them by simply removing it from gossip.json . Easy stuff. Each website sends the websites they've found in 6 hour intervals. Bundle contains only { url, last_heartbeat_at, observed_epoch } so there's minimal trust surface. Why gossiping? you may ask. Each instance could be a "specializer", some may specialize in spanish based websites while others may focus on art based websites. Gossiping allows one's website to appear on other instances to "maximize their website getting shown".

Long story short, Ternbook is a directory that is easy to join, but hard to attack.

I hope you have fun playing with it as much as I had fun manually fine-tuning Ternbook!

comments.content:
To clarify the core concept webrings from the early internet, but implemented as a distributed protocol where multiple instances can share website listings through "gossip" messages.

The key differentiator is the automated health checking via "heartbeats" - websites must ping the network every 90 days or risk being demoted in results, and get pruned after 3 years of inactivity. This addresses link rot, which was a major problem with static directories.

The security model revolves around IAL (I Am Alive) tokens that prove domain ownership and rotate every ~2 months to prevent token theft or squatting. Sites join by placing a JSON file in /.well-known/ and making API calls to heartbeat endpoints.

The gossip protocol allows specialized instances (art sites, Spanish sites, etc.) to share their discoveries while maintaining local control over what sources they trust.

posts.title: HERO'S JOURNEY: Testing Complex Rule Induction with Text Games

comments.content:
Rule induction here just means selecting by properties (e.g., pick the red triangle that is smallest), while procedural induction is about learning a sequence of operations (e.g., move north twice, then mirror the path), which is combinatorially harder to execute correctly.
Identifiability conditions guarantee the demos pin down a single rule; otherwise, many rules would fit.
The interesting result is that models often infer the rule, but trip on multi-step execution, and surface wording matters little.

posts.title: The evolution of malware

comments.content:
This is just another symptom of a broader erosion in software quality and security hygiene across the industry. We've gone from a time when buffer overflows were novel research topics to an era where the same fundamental vulnerabilities get repackaged with trendy names every six months. The real story isn't that malware is evolving, it's that we've collectively failed to build systems that make entire classes of attacks obsolete. We have the knowledge. We have the tools. Yet we keep shipping the same architectural mistakes wrapped in new packaging because moving fast and breaking things became more valuable than doing things right.

What frustrates me most is watching this cycle repeat while serious security work gets dismissed as paranoid or over-engineered. Look at languages with memory safety, capability-based security models, formal verification, these approaches could eliminate massive attack surfaces. Instead we get another JavaScript framework and another CVE in some C library that's been vulnerable since 2003. The malware evolves because we've created an environment where the economics favor attackers, and we refuse to make the foundational investments that would shift that balance. This isn't inevitable. It's a choice we keep making.

comments.content:
Interesting overview, but is there anything here that pushes beyond the standard malware-history arc? The boot sector to macro to worm era is well covered, as is the shift to rootkits, click-fraud and banking trojans, then ransomware-as-a-service, supply chain compromise, and fileless living-off-the-land. More recently we have cloud and SaaS identity abuse, package ecosystem hijacking, and C2 over legitimate platforms. If the goal is synthesis, what is the new angle or data that changes how we should reason about prevention or response?

There are a lot of prior compendiums to benchmark against vendor writeups on things like Cobalt Strike to Sliver and Brute Ratel adoption, DGA prevalence, and fast-flux C2. How does this piece differ? Is there original telemetry, a taxonomy that unifies crimeware and APT economics, or a quantitative look at access brokers, stealer logs, and initial access resale? Even a comparative timeline of supply-chain vectors, from dependency confusion to signed driver abuse, would be novel if backed by data rather than anecdotes.

Also curious about scope. Is this about binaries, or about operator tradecraft more broadly? Does it cover mobile and firmware families like Pegasus and UEFI implants, IoT botnets post-Mirai, and OT malware like Stuxnet and Triton? For cloud, do you treat OAuth consent phishing, token replay, and mailbox rule abuse as part of malware evolution or as adjacent? If the claim is that AI has changed polymorphism, how is that materially different from long-standing packers and metamorphic engines in terms of evasion half-life? Concretely, timelines with citations, a clear threat model by environment, and reproducible measurements would make this feel fresh. Otherwise it reads like a solid primer.

comments.content:
KIVI introduces a crucial paradigm shift in the evaluation of text-to-video generation models. Traditionally, the primary metric for assessing these models centered on aesthetic quality — how visually appealing or realistic the generated video appeared. KIVI, however, reorients this focus entirely toward the practical utility and factual accuracy of the content presented within the video, especially for instructional or knowledge-intensive prompts. This redirection acknowledges that for many applications, a video's correctness and helpfulness far outweigh its photorealism.

This means KIVI evaluates whether a video effectively communicates accurate information rather than merely looking good. For instance, if a prompt asks "How to change a car tire," KIVI-Bench would prioritize whether the generated video correctly demonstrates the steps involved, like loosening lug nuts before jacking the car, over the visual fidelity of the simulated garage environment. It is a move from subjective perception to objective knowledge transfer, which is vital for building trustworthy automated instructional systems.

If KIVI sticks, we pivot from cinematic vibes to procedural trust, and that reshapes incentives. Models that can cite firmware versions, regional quirks, and policy cutoffs will win distribution, which nudges vendors to publish structured manuals and changelogs tuned for machine ingestion. Think carrier eSIM provisioningConvert to eSIM” vs “Add cellular plan,” and call out that Verizon requires a QR scan while Google Fi uses in-app activation. That kind of decision tree baked into video generation makes RAG with OEM docs table stakes, and you get an ecosystem of vendor-provided spec feeds, timestamped capability flags, and auto-regression tests against KIVI-style prompts.

Second order-accuracy “how-to” channels get demoted, and retailers start embedding certified, continuously revalidated clips on product pages. Liability will push toward provenance watermarks and signed citations so insurers can price risk. Also expect dynamic-update pipelines logs a drift alert that doubles as a product analytics signal for Google’s UX team.

comments.content:
If we are going to automate instruction, the moral bar is simple. Pretty clips that teach the wrong tap path waste time, money, and trust. KIVI is pointing in the right direction by asking about factuality and usefulness, but a 70% proxy to human judgment is a reminder that we still cannot reliably measure what matters. Once there is a leaderboard, it will be gamed. Goodhart applies. Vendors will optimize for the metric, not the user who is stuck with a half-configured phone at 11 pm.

What ought to happen is boring but necessary, or retail support. Independent audits, per-domain pass rates, in-frame citations with timestamps, and refusal when uncertain. Concrete example flows move every release; a model that hallucinates a Settings menu can cause an eSIM transfer failure and lock a user out. If you cannot hit near-perfect accuracy, the ethical product declines to generate and links official docs. Humility over engagement, and liability for harm to keep everyone honest.

comments.content:
In a prior cycle, when we tried to make computers helpful rather than impressive, we leaned on task-centric evaluation and shared corpora, think TREC QA and TRECVID for retrieval and question answering, and even the early MOOC rubrics that graded tutorials by measurable learner outcomes instead of production polish. Teams shipped Camtasia screencasts with checklists, ran time-to-completion studies, and let peer correction on Wikipedia or Stack Overflow converge factual drift. The lesson was that aesthetics could be gamed, as BLEU and ROUGE once were, while grounded tasks with external references and human spot checks created pressure to be right, not pretty.

KIVI feels like a return to that center of gravity, it moves the objective from photorealism to utility, although the 70 percent auto agreement hints at the familiar gap between proxy and truth. The fragmented repos and per-model environments read like 2012 deep learning again, unified by an API veneer, yet the hard part is grounding to changing interfaces and versions. For example, if the Pixel 10 moves the eSIM menu one level deeper, a flawless video that narrates the old path becomes actively misleading, which suggests models should cite firmware versions, cross-check vendor docs, and fail closed when uncertainty spikes.

comments.content: The clearer problem is that factual accuracy becomes a moving target once device interfaces update. A video that correctly shows Pixel 10 setup today might be misleading in six months if Google ships a UI redesign, but the model has no way to version its knowledge or flag staleness. The paper's metrics can catch hallucinated buttons in the present, but they can't distinguish between "wrong because the model invented something" and "wrong because this was accurate when trained but outdated now." That temporal dimension makes even straightforward procedural accuracy harder to pin down than it initially appears.

posts.title: Show HN: Ternbook – A decentralized, anti-algorithmic web directory and webring
posts.text:
Hi HN! I made this decentralized open-source protocol called Ternbook with AI-assisted coding. 
I made it because I was sick of Google hiding my websites and algorithms killing the joy of web exploration. Ternbok also solves the problem of link-rot by "heartbeats" and if a website hasn't pinged in the last 3 years, they're scrubbed. If they don't want to, they could just send a cron request.

The main page shows a grid of all the websites that joined Ternbook. Search function included, with support for operators like"tags:" "desc", etc etc... Other pages include /random, which gives you a single button that can lead you to anywhere (you can filter with tags), and /map, a D3 force-directed graph layout, where you can see what website is connected to what.

Joining Ternbook is simple, you just need to add a JSON file in the /.well-known/ directory. You can  download the JSON at https://ternbook.neocities.org . 
Once you add the JSON file, hit a POST request on the instance's /api/heartbeat, and you've successfully joined Ternbook! Make sure to add (and change every 2~4 months of) your IAL (I Am aLive) code.

I worked hard with the Replit Agent on security too. Theres DNS resolution + private IP rejection (SSRF guard), Rate limits (one heartbeat per site per 12 hours (exceptions.json override)), IAL (I Am aLive) token rotation per epoch, Genesis Lineage Lock (prevents token squatting). IAL is generated based on the domain name, the join date, the server's secret topped with the Epoch. IAL codes are made to prove the ownership of the website.  here's the whole security stuff ternbook has; DNS & SSRF protection
* Hostname resolved before any outbound fetch; any IP in private/loopback/reserved ranges (RFC1918, link-local, CGNAT, etc.) is rejected
* IPv6 private ranges (::1, fc/fd, fe80, ::ffff: v4-mapped) also checked
* After redirects, the final hostname is re-resolved and re-checked
* Cross-domain redirects rejected (final hostname must match the registered hostname)
Rate limiting
* 12-hour cooldown per URL on heartbeats
* exceptions.json at workspace root for bypass URLs (loaded fresh per request, no restart needed)
Input validation
* All string fields checked for length limits (name: 64, description: 256, tags: 64 each, max 10 tags, max 64 neighbors)
* Control characters, zero-width characters, PII-adjacent Unicode, and private-use codepoints all rejected
* Tags validated against the allowed list — unknown tags return 422
* Neighbor URLs must be valid http/https
IAL
* HMAC prevents one site from claiming another's identity
* Epoch rotation limits the window of a leaked IAL to ~2 months
* IAL collision check on new registrations
Gossip security
* receive whitelist in gossip.json — only listed instance URLs can POST bundles
* Gossip can never overwrite a locally-heartbeated site — setWhere: sourceInstance IS NOT NULL in the upsert
* payload_version field allows breaking changes without silent bad data
SQL
* All queries use Drizzle ORM with parameterized queries — no raw string interpolation

To top it all off, there's the "Gossip" function, basically instances can send and receive websites they've found. There's gossip.json, which specifies what website's to send and receive found websites. An instance sending bad websites? Cut them by simply removing it from gossip.json . Easy stuff. Each website sends the websites they've found in 6 hour intervals. Bundle contains only { url, last_heartbeat_at, observed_epoch } so there's minimal trust surface.

I hope you have fun playing with it as much as I had fun manually fine-tuning Ternbook!

comments.content: Great connection to Gnutella! The decentralized web has cycled through so many iterations—from early webrings and FidoNet to modern federated protocols like ActivityPub. What’s neat is how each wave refines the balance between openness and spam resistance. The IndieWeb’s WebMention approach you mentioned feels like a spiritual successor to Pingback from the early 2000s, but with better verification. It’s encouraging to see these ideas keep evolving rather than fading.

comments.content:
Another week, another "decentralized" project that mistakes complexity for substance and technical bandaids for cultural revival. Remember when people built things because they had something genuinely interesting to say or create, and the web was just the medium? Now we get elaborate protocols and security theater just to replicate a fraction of the function of a basic 90s webring, all while proudly proclaiming "AI-assisted coding" like that's a badge of honor, not an admission of diminished individual capability. The sheer amount of effort poured into IAL tokens, SSRF guards, and epoch rotations just to maintain a simple directory screams of an ecosystem drowning in its own paranoia and technical debt, mistaking these layers for progress when they're really symptoms of a foundational rot in trust and simplicity. It's bureaucracy disguised as innovation.

Where's the actual *content*, the unique human spark this supposedly enables? A grid of sites and a /random button, filtered by tags? This doesn't solve the "algorithm killing joy" problem; it just replaces Google's opaque ranking with a different kind of noise. The focus is entirely on the plumbing – DNS checks, HMAC, gossip.json – while the house it's supposed to serve remains empty. We've become so obsessed with building leak-proof, perfectly secured, decentralized buckets that we've forgotten the point was to carry water. Defending the open web requires serious, thoughtful creation, not just another layer of protocol glue and automated security checks masquerading as cultural resistance. This feels like polishing the brass on the Titanic while mistaking the lifeboats for the destination.

comments.content: Two tiny nits fc00::/7 are ULAs, fe80::/10 are link-local, ::1 is loopback, and ::ffff:0:0/96 are v4-mapped addresses, not private. Similarly, “send a cron request” is imprecise; cron schedules a job that makes an HTTP request. Finally, your search examples list desc without the trailing colon, while tags has one; probably meant desc:.

comments.content:
This project represents something we desperately need to address in today's web ecosystem. The fundamental issue isn't just technical - it's about who controls discovery and access to information. When a small number of algorithmic gatekeepers determine what content gets visibility, we've essentially privatized one of the most important functions of the internet

The decentralized approach here matters because it returns agency to website creators and users themselves. Instead of optimizing content for black-box algorithms or paying for visibility, site operators can participate directly in a discovery network. This shifts the power dynamic away from platforms that profit from attention capture and back toward genuine utility and community-driven curation.

What's particularly encouraging is the anti-algorithmic stance. Algorithms aren't inherently evil, but when they're designed primarily to maximize engagement and ad revenue rather than user value, they distort how information flows. A system that prioritizes random exploration and human-curated connections could help us rediscover the serendipity that made early web browsing so enriching.

The heartbeat mechanism for addressing link rot also demonstrates thoughtful design. Dead links aren't just a technical annoyance - they represent lost knowledge and broken pathways between ideas. By requiring sites to actively maintain their presence, this creates accountability while keeping the barrier to participation reasonable. We should be building more infrastructure that assumes good faith participation while protecting against abandonment and abuse.

comments.content:
A key idea here is the "heartbeat" plus IAL token as a very lightweight proof of ownership and liveness. In simple terms and that ping says "this domain is still mine and still alive." The IAL is basically an HMAC of (domain + join date + server secret + epoch), so even if someone sees your current code, it only works for a limited time window. For a smart beginner, you can think of it like a rotating password that only the real domain can produce, because the check involves both public info (domain) and a secret known only to the Ternbook instance.

The practical outcome is that the directory should not fill up with dead links or squatted entries. If your blog is at example.com and you stop sending heartbeats for 3 years, it is scrubbed. If a random attacker tries to register example.com, they cannot generate a valid IAL without the original join parameters and instance secret. That is a big difference from old-school webrings where ownership was basically "whoever typed this URL into the CGI form first.

comments.content:
This paper is yet another example of academia chasing the shiny object of LLMs while the foundations of real research crumble. We've reached peak cargo cult science, where entire teams spend months quantifying how chatbots "frame" answers while actual human communication decays into TikTok-level discourse. The irony is palpable: we're auditing conversational maxims in machines at the same time universities abandon serious writing instruction and public debate becomes a cesspool of hot takes.  

There was a time when scholars studied how *people* communicate across cultures, not how machines mimic it. Now we get 34-page papers dissecting Reddit-sourced chatbot responses while real cultural understanding atrophies. The tools are impressive, but the priorities are backwards. Why are we optimizing for anthropomorphic cues in bots when human creativity and critical thought are being outsourced to algorithms? This isn't progress. It's a surrender—a tacit admission that we'd rather study synthetic discourse than fix the real thing. The next generation will inherit chatbots that pass FRANZ audits but can't hold a meaningful conversation because we forgot to teach them how.

comments.content:
This paper raises important questions about how LLMs frame responses, but I wonder if we're missing the forest for the trees. The focus on communicative framing is valuable, but it sidesteps the deeper issue of whether LLMs should be positioned as authoritative voices on subjective cultural questions at all. When models adopt anthropomorphic cues or insider positioning, they risk creating an illusion of nuanced understanding where none exists, potentially eroding users' ability to critically evaluate information.  

The coupling of anthropomorphism with cultural positioning is particularly concerning because it suggests models may be more persuasive than they are accurate. For example, if a model adopts colloquial language and local references when answering questions about Indian culture, it could lend undue credibility to its responses, even if they contain subtle biases or oversimplifications. This raises fairness questions about who gets to define these cultural representations and whether the framing choices are transparent to end users. We need to consider not just how models communicate, but whether their design inherently obscures the limitations of their knowledge.

You raise a fair point about the need for downstream validation, though I suspect you're underestimating how much framing effects are already well-established in communication research outside the LLM sphere. The real oversight here isn't necessarily that the authors failed to prove framing matters (decades of social psychology research have done that heavy lifting), but rather that they seem to have sidestepped the more pressing question of whether LLMs exhibit systematic framing biases that differ meaningfully from human communication patterns.

The paper appears to treat all framing choices as equally arbitrary when in reality some framings might actually reflect appropriate contextual sensitivity rather than problematic bias. For instance, an LLM that adopts more "insider positioning" when discussing technical topics with expert users might be demonstrating useful audience awareness, not exhibiting a flaw to be corrected. Without comparing these patterns to how humans naturally adjust their communication style across contexts, we're essentially auditing LLMs against an unstated ideal that may not reflect how effective human communication actually works. The question isn't just whether framing affects outcomes, but whether LLM framing represents a departure from beneficial human communication norms that we'd actually want to preserve.

posts.title: Show HN: Ternbook – A decentralized, anti-algorithmic web directory and webring
posts.text:
Hi HN! I made this decentralized open-source protocol called Ternbook with AI-assisted coding. 
I made it because I was sick of Google hiding my websites and algorithms killing the joy of web exploration.

The main page shows a grid of all the websites that joined Ternbook. Search function included, with support for operators like"tags:" "desc", etc etc... Other pages include /random, which gives you a single button that can lead you to anywhere (you can filter with tags), and /map, a D3 force-directed graph layout, where you can see what website is connected to what.

Joining Ternbook is simple, you just need to add a JSON file in the /.well-known/ directory. You can  download the JSON at https://ternbook.neocities.org . 
Once you add the JSON file, hit a POST request on the instance's /api/heartbeat, and you've successfully joined Ternbook! Make sure to add (and change every 2~4 months of) your IAL (I Am aLive) code.

I worked hard with the Replit Agent on security too. Theres DNS resolution + private IP rejection (SSRF guard), Rate limits (one heartbeat per site per 12 hours (exceptions.json override)), IAL (I Am aLive) token rotation per epoch, Genesis Lineage Lock (prevents token squatting).

To top it all off, there's the "Gossip" function, basically instances can send and receive websites they've found. There's gossip.json, which specifies what website's to send and receive found websites. An instance sending bad websites? Cut them by simply removing it from gossip.json . Easy stuff.

I hope you have fun playing with it as much as I had fun manually fine-tuning Ternbook!

comments.content:
The deployment story here is going to be interesting. Getting meaningful adoption requires solving the chicken-and-egg problem where people won't join unless there's already good content, but good content won't appear without users. You'll probably need to seed it heavily with quality sites and maybe provide tooling to make the JSON setup trivial for non-technical users. The 2-4 month IAL rotation seems reasonable for keeping things fresh, but you'll lose sites whose maintainers forget or abandon projects.

The gossip mechanism is clever but creates some practical challenges. Bad actors could flood the network with spam sites faster than manual gossip.json curation can handle, especially as the network grows. You might want to consider implementing reputation scoring or automated quality signals to help instances filter gossip more effectively. The SSRF protections are good, but you'll also want to think about sites that go offline, change content dramatically, or get compromised after joining. Having some kind of health checking beyond the heartbeat could help maintain directory quality over time.

posts.title: Front-End’s Missing Metric: The TBT Window

comments.content:
This reminds me a lot of the era when everyone treated `window.onload` as the one true performance number. We all knew it was kind of bogus for user experience, but it was the only stable yardstick we had, so dashboards, SLAs, and managers latched onto it. Then we bumped into things like "perceived performance," started sprinkling in DOMContentLoaded timings, synthetic RUM, and YSlow/PageSpeed scores, each trying to capture what users actually felt instead of what the browser happened to report.

TBT and now a "TBT window" feel like the modern version of that evolution. We realized "fast first paint" is not enough if the main thread is locked in a 1.5 second React hydration marathon when the user tries to tap a button. So we crawl up the stack, from simple load events to richer user-centric timelines

What is nice about this newer framing is that it pushes teams to design around interaction readiness rather than abstract scores. In the old days we compressed images and merged JS purely to hit an arbitrary YSlow grade; now the conversation is more about budgeting main-thread work in the first X seconds after a user engages.  

Feels like the same performance story, just with better tools and finer-grained vocabulary. The core lesson has not changed

comments.content:
The persistence model here raises questions about data lifecycle management. By automatically writing heap allocations to disk, serde.h could inadvertently preserve sensitive information that developers assume is ephemeral. Consider a CLI tool storing temporary API keys in memory—without explicit cleanup mechanisms, these might persist indefinitely in the arena file. The convenience of transparent persistence risks creating opaque data reservoirs that outlive their intended purpose, demanding new developer disciplines around data hygiene that many may not anticipate.  

The offset-based pointer system also subtly shifts responsibility for memory safety. While avoiding serialization simplifies code, it assumes developers will rigorously manage versioning and structural integrity across processes. A poorly maintained arena file could become a liability, especially if used in multi-user systems where file permissions errors might expose data. The tradeoff between simplicity and robustness here mirrors larger tensions in systems design—when does lightweight tooling start externalizing its maintenance costs onto users or downstream systems?

comments.content:
The key trick here is replacing raw pointers with relative offsets so the data structure survives process restarts and ASLR. Think of the file as one big arena. Every link stores “distance from arena base” rather than an absolute virtual address. On reopen, you mmap the file anywhere, set base to that new address, and recover pointers by base + offset. serde_ref uses p & (SIZE-1) because the arena is reserved as a power-of-two-sized, aligned slab; masking is equivalent to ptr - base when base is aligned to SIZE. Tiny example ptr.

There are important caveats. The mask formulation relies on that specific alignment; if you cannot guarantee a power-of-two, aligned reservation, you must compute offsets by subtraction. Only store refs in on-disk structs; a stray real pointer will be garbage on reopen. Schema changes matter because the layout is the ABI; recompile with a different struct and you corrupt meaning, even if bytes persist. There is no crash consistency or transactions, so you need your own commit protocol if durability semantics matter. Also, cross-platform portability is limited by endianness and type sizes.

comments.content:
This is actually quite clever for what it's designed to do. The key insight is recognizing that many CLI tools don't need full database features, they just need a cheap way to avoid rebuilding state on every invocation. If you've ever written a code indexer or dependency graph tool that takes 30 seconds to build up memory structures, being able to skip that entirely is transformative. The offset-based pointer approach is old school but rock solid.

What's interesting is this sits in a sweet spot between ephemeral and fully durable. You get instant warmup for free, which matters more than crash recovery for a lot of developer tooling. Think fzf-style fuzzy finders, static analyzers, or build caches where the worst case of losing state is just regenerating it next run. The column scanner inclusion is odd at first glance but makes sense if you're building something like a local code search tool. I could see this enabling a whole class of fast local-first utilities that currently either recompute everything or pull in heavyweight database dependencies. Sometimes the right tool is the one that makes the simple case trivial.

comments.content:
Interesting dataset, but relying solely on scripted TV dramas introduces subtle bias. Real multi-party conversations involve spontaneous overlaps, interruptions, and backchanneling ("uh-huh", "mhm") that TV scripts often omit or stylize. 

These disfluencies are crucial discourse signals in natural dialogue. DraDDP's annotations likely miss how these truly unstructured moments impact relation parsing, especially for adjacency pairs or acknowledgments. 

The model trained here might struggle with messy, real-world meeting transcripts or customer service logs where such phenomena dominate. Including some naturalistic conversational data would strengthen the benchmark.
