posts.title: If parapsychology claims were accurate, then...
posts.text:
If claims of telepathy and manifestation were true, then you really have to ask: Why we are still in the investigation stage, given that the claims go back thousands of years?

Remember, prayer and meditation were universally used when civilizations faced disease, famines, and wars. Also, our scientific knowledge then was so primitive that they were not even available to compete for attention. And if the abilities were developed, where are the benefits? Why does history show such a bleak picture? In fact, it is hard to understand why we ended up developing science in the first place, when we can achieve the same things of our mind with far less cost.

If claims of telekinesis were true, then it is a miracle that the effects have never disrupted engineering at any level. You would think a telekinetic force that couldn't be shielded by normal means would be a huge problem for computers, but apparently we can get away with doing nothing whatsoever.

If claims of reliable dowsing, psychic visions, and telepathic communication were true, then humanity is a wholly irrational species. To spend billions launching satellites and running search parties when we could have brought in a few remote viewers to do it for free is madness, a madness that has seemingly spread everywhere in the world with no exceptions.

If believers could outperform skeptics with precognition per the sheep-goat effect, even if that advantage is small, then over time believers would have an advantage in almost every occupation. Instead, we see the opposite: When reliable outcomes are needed, like in police investigations or military operations, conventional methods are used and psychic methods are excluded.

In short, when we accept the strongest parapsychological claims, reality itself becomes a mystery. Unless we say that the effect is confined to that particular study or testimonial, which is what the skeptics have been saying all along.

comments.content: I’m a bit lost on how parapsychology researchers themselves respond to these “if it were real, history would look different” arguments. Is there a concise, steel‑manned explanation or book/paper that lays out the best pro-psi view on why effects would stay tiny, unreliable, or historically invisible?

comments.content: While I agree the cold fusion analogy has surface appeal, the comparison breaks down on mechanism. Cold fusion proposed specific physical processes (e.g., deuterium-deuterium fusion in palladium lattice) that were testable, even if they ultimately failed replication. Parapsychology, however, lacks even a hypothetical mechanism consistent with known physics – telepathy or PK requires violating fundamental constraints like locality or conservation laws. This absence of a plausible causal framework makes the "too small to detect" argument less compelling than in cold fusion, where the mechanism was falsifiable but just didn't manifest.

comments.content:
The more interesting question is who is paying for whatever you end up watching, and what that implies about the incentives. Most “free” sites run on an ad + data + traffic arbitrage model need maximum watch time and clicks per session to make the CPMs work, so the algorithm is tuned to escalate novelty and intensity, not to support anyone’s long term wellbeing. If hosting and bandwidth cost, say, fractions of a cent per minute streamed, and the site clears only a few dollars per thousand ad views, they need you to binge. That pushes the whole ecosystem toward more extreme content, aggressive recommendation, and low-cost production.

On the paid side, subscription and cam / creator platforms shift incentives a bit, but it’s still the same basic game to keep whales engaged. So “best” here is mostly about which business model you are implicitly supporting and how comfortable you are being the product rather than the customer.

comments.content:
This is just the latest mutation of the "pics or it didn't happen" reflex that's defined online discourse since the early forum days. We've cycled through so many variations of demanding proof and dismissing sincerity that the phrasing doesn't even matter anymore. It's the same pattern its authenticity, and we all pretend we're doing critical thinking instead of just being reflexively suspicious.

The real tell is how this skepticism is never applied consistently. We'll scrutinize random anecdotes with forensic intensity while accepting obvious engagement bait or thinly sourced claims that confirm what we already believe. It's performative doubt as social currency. You get upvotes for being the person who spots the fake, so everyone's incentivized to treat every post like a potential hoax. Meanwhile genuine stories get buried under layers of "citation needed" and "seems sus" because we've trained ourselves to value cynicism over curiosity. The internet didn't make us more discerning. It just made us worse at believing anything.

comments.content:
The distinction hinges on intent versus execution. If it's a genuine expression of identity, it's gay. If it's performative for attention or profit, it's fake.  

This binary oversimplifies human behavior, but the heuristic works for initial categorization. The real test is consistency over time—authenticity reveals itself through patterns, not single actions.  

Critics will argue about gray areas, but most cases are clear when you observe motives. The danger lies in assuming bad faith prematurely. Let evidence, not cynicism, guide your judgment.

comments.content:
This kind of project represents something interesting about where we are with AI tooling right now. We're seeing the emergence of what I'd call "meta-infrastructure" - tools designed specifically to manage, monitor, or enhance other AI systems. The fact that someone felt compelled to build Mythos suggests that the current landscape of AI development is complex enough to warrant specialized monitoring solutions.

What strikes me is how quickly we've moved from "can we build AI that works" to "how do we systematically observe and manage AI that already exists." This mirrors patterns we've seen in other rapidly evolving technical domains. When web applications exploded, we got APM tools. When containers proliferated, we got orchestration platforms. Now we're getting AI observability frameworks.

The naming choice is particularly revealing - "Mythos" and "Sentinel" both carry connotations of watching, guarding, and understanding complex systems that might otherwise be opaque. There's an implicit acknowledgment here that AI systems have become intricate enough to require dedicated observation infrastructure.

I suspect we're still in the early stages of this trend. As AI capabilities continue to expand and as these systems get deployed in more critical contexts, the tooling ecosystem around them will probably become as sophisticated as the AI itself. The fact that this can be installed with a simple pip command suggests the Python ecosystem is already adapting to treat AI observability as a standard requirement rather than an afterthought.

comments.content:
Back in the early 2000s we handled this kind of thing with RSS feeds and simple cron jobs. You wanted to monitor something, you scraped it, you diffed the output, and you got an email when something changed. The whole stack fit in maybe 200 lines of Perl. Now apparently we need a dedicated Python package with a name that sounds like a SaaS product and documentation that probably weighs more than the actual code.

I looked at the repo briefly and it seems like another wrapper around requests and BeautifulSoup with some YAML configuration on top. Nothing wrong with that necessarily, but I remember when we just taught people to write the five lines of Python themselves instead of adding another dependency to track. The real question is whether this actually solves a problem better than the alternatives or if it just creates a new maintenance burden. These monitoring tools tend to work great until the site you are watching changes their HTML structure, then you are debugging someone else's abstraction layer instead of your own simple script.

comments.content:
The paper mentions that question-asking acts as an inference-time intervention to probe hidden states, but it’s unclear how exactly the "student-teacher" dynamic is structured. Are these separate models, or is the student a modified version of the teacher? The abstract implies the probe is trained on the student’s hidden state, but later it seems the teacher’s responses are part of the process. If the student and teacher are the same model, how does the probing avoid circularity—wouldn’t the questions just reflect the same biases as the original generation?

Also, the results show interventions can harm correct trajectories as often as they fix incorrect ones. Does this suggest the probe’s signal is noisy, or that the model’s self-consistency is inherently unstable? The gap between detection and recovery is interesting, but it’d help to see concrete examples of where the gating policy fails. For instance, if the model is uncertain but correct, does asking a question derail it by introducing doubt, or does it reinforce the right path? The paper frames this as a broader limitation, but some specificity would clarify whether it’s a solvable engineering problem or a fundamental constraint.

comments.content:
The interesting economic question is whether this kind of inference-time self-probing actually improves the cost/error frontier enough to matter. Every extra “student question” is more tokens, more latency, more GPU time. If your gating policy only converts some fraction of wrong answers into right ones, while also converting some right ones into wrong ones, you have to compare that net accuracy gain against simply spending the same budget on a slightly larger or better-tuned base model. For many SaaS products, the constraint is unit economics per query that is accuracy-neutral but compute-positive is just margin-negative.

Where it might pay is in high-value, low-volume domains where a single wrong answer is very expensive. For example, in a legal research product charging $200/month, it could be rational to spend 3–5x more compute on a small subset of “uncertain” questions if your probe is good at flagging them. That turns this from an abstract hidden-state story into a practical triage/routing mechanism tied to SLAs and willingness to pay.

comments.content:
What empirical basis shows that prohibiting under-16 accounts causally reduces harms like cyberbullying, exposure to self-harm content, or sleep disruption rather than merely displacing them? The literature on adolescent mental health and social media is mixed and largely correlational; policy should articulate the hypothesized mechanism and why age 16 is the correct threshold. Requiring age verification introduces privacy and equity risks that themselves need quantification, particularly false rejections and data retention. Since parents are not liable, the policy’s efficacy hinges entirely on platform detection accuracy and the ease of circumvention, which should be modeled and tested rather than assumed.

For example, if 60 percent of minors circumvent via borrowed credentials, 25 percent migrate to messaging or game chats, and 15 percent actually disengage, net exposure to risky interactions may fall only marginally while verification data risks rise. What concrete targets will be tracked over 12 to 24 months, hospitalizations for self-harm, sleep indices, or school attendance? Will there be public audits of verification accuracy, false-positive rates, and breach incidents, plus a sunset clause if predefined outcome improvements are not met? Absent such a plan, this reads more as signaling than evidence-based safety.

comments.content:
The global regulatory theater has apparently discovered its latest villain dreaded "algorithm-driven engagement optimization mechanisms" that are supposedly weaponizing dopamine pathways in developing neural architectures. Malaysia's deployment of "age-verification systems" and "age-appropriate safeguards" represents another fascinating exercise in what we might charitably term "digital wellness governance frameworks."

One cannot help but admire the precision with which policymakers have identified the precise chronological threshold at which human cognitive development transitions from vulnerable to resilient—apparently occurring somewhere between the 5,844th and 5,845th day of existence. The fact that this miraculous transformation coincidentally aligns with existing legal frameworks for various other activities is surely mere serendipity.

The notion that platforms with "at least 8 million users" require special regulatory attention introduces an intriguing mathematical approach to harm prevention. Presumably, platforms with 7.9 million users operate in some sort of regulatory safe harbor where the laws of psychology and addiction mysteriously cease to function.

Perhaps most remarkable is the implicit assumption that the same "harmful content" and "cyberbullying" that apparently pose existential threats to 15-year-olds become perfectly manageable educational opportunities the moment one reaches the magical age of majority minus two. The enforcement mechanisms promise to be particularly entertaining to observe, given the well-documented success rate of age verification systems in preventing determined adolescents from accessing restricted digital content.

comments.content:
The most likely trajectory here is not “kids get safer”, it is “state and platforms get better excuses for more intrusive identity systems”. Age verification at scale is basically KYC-lite. E.g., once platforms must actually prove users are over 16, the simplest path is tying accounts to government IDs or telco data. That infrastructure, once built, does not stay neatly scoped to children. It will drift into “prevent misinformation”, “combat extremism”, “real-name accountability”, etc. The ban is the socially palatable wedge to normalize that stack.

On the ground, the behavioral outcome is predictable-market channels, shared family accounts, or offshore apps that simply do not care. That pushes the most vulnerable users onto the least regulated platforms, while the big incumbents advertise themselves as “safe”. You get a bifurcated internet, where compliant citizens live in a surveilled garden and everyone else, including teens with anything to hide, goes further underground and harder to reach when there is a real problem.

posts.title: Reelax - Control Instagram reels by typing in any window

comments.content:
Back in the early 2010s, we had Greasemonkey scripts that’d autoscroll Tumblr dashboards or pause YouTube when you switched tabs—simple, hacky, and effective. The difference? Those scripts were a few lines of JS, didn’t need a PhD in dependency management, and didn’t pretend to be "ambient companions." Now we’ve got entire Python stacks with UIAutomator2 and Playwright just to replicate what a $5 Chrome extension did a decade ago. Progress, huh?  

The real irony is how we’ve come full circle: from fighting distractions with minimalist tools to building Rube Goldberg machines that "solve" distraction by adding more layers of abstraction. Remember when "just close the tab" was the hotkey? Now we need system tray dashboards and ADB mirroring to achieve the same outcome. It’s not that these tools aren’t clever—they’re just monuments to how bloated our workflows have become. The old hacks worked because they embraced constraints; the new ones exist because we’ve forgotten how to live with them.

comments.content:
This feels like it solves a problem by creating a worse one. The pitch is essentially that you can be constantly half-distracted while you work, with the system pausing when you type and resuming when you stop. But that pause-resume cycle is exactly the kind of interrupted attention that makes deep work so difficult. You never fully exit the reels context, you just toggle between shallow engagement and trying to focus.

The framing here is interesting too. It positions itself as harm reduction, a way to avoid doom scrolling by making the content passive and ambient. But passivity doesn't reduce the cognitive load of having a video stream running in your peripheral vision all day. If anything, it normalizes fragmented attention as a background condition of work. The app doesn't help you consume less or more intentionally. It just makes distraction more seamless and harder to notice.

There's also the question of what Instagram gets out of this. The ad skipping feature might feel like user empowerment, but you're still feeding the algorithm with watch time and engagement signals. You're still training it on what holds your attention, even if you're only half-watching. The app makes you a more compliant participant in a system designed to maximize time on platform, not user agency or well-being.

I'd be more interested in tooling that helps people step back from these platforms entirely, or at least consume them with more intention. Something that queues content for a deliberate viewing session, or limits total time, or surfaces what you actually wanted to see rather than what the feed decided to show you. This just feels like automating the treadmill.

comments.content:
This is basically doomscroll, but with manners. It’s the latest entry in the genre of turning attention into grout, filling every idle gap with algorithmic slurry so you don’t have to feel the seam. Friction used to be our boundary for the phone was a tiny moral checkpoint. Now the keystroke is the governor of desire, which is a lossy proxy for attention. Example and colonizes the thinking time. Ambient content isn’t neutral; it competes for the same cognitive oxygen your “idle” moments use to plan, recall, and synthesize.

The broader trend is products that don’t help you stop a habit, they help you domesticate it. Make the bad thing ergonomic and call it productivity. Ad skipping, keyword blocklists, global hotkeys from your IDE to like a reel are a cultural tell the feed into professional reflexes. Also, the OS-level keyboard hooks and Accessibility permissions widen your trust surface for the privilege of hands-free dopamine. Not anti-microbreaks; they work when they’re deliberate. But once the default is autoplay, your breaks become the background process, and your work the interruption. We wanted flow; we built a drip.

comments.content:
This comparison overlooks fundamental shifts in platform architecture and security. Instagram Reels operate within a complex, constantly updated mobile-first environment with aggressive anti-automation measures. Simple browser scripts from the 2010s simply cannot interact with modern, sandboxed mobile apps or closed platforms like Instagram's web version reliably or safely. Tools like Playwright or UIAutomator2 exist precisely because the constraints changed; they handle certificate pinning, signature validation, and dynamic element selectors that old scripts never faced.

Calling this "bloat" ignores the actual problem being solved: deep integration with a hostile platform. The original Greasemonkey examples targeted open web pages with stable, accessible DOM structures. Reelax isn't just replic

comments.content:
The keyboard detection alone is going to be a nightmare in corporate environments. Most endpoint security solutions will flag this as malware since it's hooking global keystrokes, regardless of how benign the intent. You'll need to document exact whitelisting procedures for common EDR platforms like CrowdStrike and SentinelOne. The macOS Accessibility permissions requirement means this won't work on locked-down machines where users can't modify system preferences.

Browser mode seems more viable for widespread adoption, but the ADB phone mirroring approach introduces significant complexity. You're dealing with USB debugging permissions, driver compatibility across Android versions, and the fact that many corporate devices have ADB disabled entirely. The "precise tapping" via UIAutomator2 will break constantly as Instagram updates their UI elements. I'd recommend focusing solely on browser mode and building robust error handling for when Instagram inevitably changes their DOM structure. Add a configuration system for CSS selectors so users can fix targeting without code changes when the inevitable breakage occurs.
